ctx.name: Sam
ctx.notes: Sam is in week three and has started picking up real tickets, but he still hesitates before shipping and asks for reassurance late. I need to understand whether this is a normal confidence dip or a tooling and expectation gap we can fix quickly. I also want clarity on where onboarding handoffs are still fuzzy so he is not quietly compensating. This should stay practical and supportive: map what is unclear, unblock him, and line up the right support touchpoints for the next two weeks.

focusPoints.label: Shipping confidence and reassurance timing.
focusPoints.reason: Week three signal: he hesitates before shipping and asks for reassurance late. Could be a normal confidence dip, or a tooling/expectation gap we can fix quickly—worth clarifying what specifically makes shipping feel risky right now.

focusPoints.label: What 'done' means in his onboarding handoffs.
focusPoints.reason: Onboarding handoffs still feel fuzzy in places. One possibility is he’s compensating by over-checking because the success bar and acceptance steps aren’t crisp yet—map the exact handoff points that are unclear so you can tighten them for the next two weeks.

focusPoints.label: Right support touchpoints for the next two weeks.
focusPoints.reason: Whether he’s a junior or not, onboarding succeeds when the manager and team make support predictable: who to ask, when to review, and what kind of reassurance is available before work reaches the “late” stage.

focusPoints.label: Tooling or process friction slowing his loop.
focusPoints.reason: Junior engineers often stall when the daily loop isn’t smooth—CI/review tooling, unclear steps, or missing context. Worth identifying the specific friction points so you can remove them quickly rather than treating it as confidence alone.

transcript.question: What's been most on your mind at work lately, Sam?
transcript.answer: I can do the tasks, but I still second-guess whether my approach matches team expectations.

transcript.question: When you second-guess your approach, what expectations feel hardest to read?
transcript.answer: Local setup is better now, but I still lose time when the design and API details do not match.

transcript.question: When design and API details do not match, where does it usually break first?
transcript.answer: I am not always sure who should approve what before I push.

transcript.question: What does 'done' mean to you on the tickets you've picked up so far?
transcript.answer: I usually wait too long before asking because I want to solve things myself first.

transcript.question: usually wait long — can you say more about what that means for you right now?
transcript.answer: When feedback is specific and fast, I move much quicker.

transcript.question: Specific and fast feedback — where is that missing most right now?
transcript.answer: I still feel fuzzy on where quality bars differ between quick fixes and core flows.

transcript.question: What tends to make shipping feel risky right before you send it?
transcript.answer: Pairing once or twice a week would probably prevent me from getting stuck silently.

transcript.question: What part of your workflow is slowing you down most right now?
transcript.answer: If we define what good looks like for my next two tickets, I will feel much steadier.

transcript.question: Given what we've covered, what would you want to tighten up first?
transcript.answer: I want to start by pairing on my next ticket so I can see the approval bar in practice.
